g.data-narration: IP Security is a framework that secures IP communications at the network layer, providing confidentiality, integrity, and authentication.
g.text: IP Security (IPSec) is a suite of protocols that secures Internet Protocol
communications by providing encryption, authentication, and integrity.

g.data-narration: IP Security protects data from unauthorized access, ensures integrity through hashing, and provides confidentiality via encryption.
An IP Security Policy defines rules for key management, tunnel mode selection, and encryption algorithms used in IPSec communications.
g.text: Definition: A set of rules that defines how IPSec protocols are used to
secure IP communications. Includes key management, tunnel mode, and encryption.

g.data-narration: Example policy uses RSA 2048-bit keys for key management, transport mode for packet encapsulation, and AES-256-CBC for encryption.
ESP provides confidentiality and integrity by encrypting the payload and adding authentication. It uses AES for encryption and HMAC-SHA-256 for authentication.
g.text: ESP protocol provides confidentiality and integrity for IP packets by
encrypting the payload and adding authentication header.

g.data-narration: An ESP packet contains SPI, Sequence Number, Encrypted Data, Padding, and Authentication Tag for secure transmission.
Combining Security Associations allows multiple protocols to work together, providing layered security with transport and tunnel modes.
g.text: Multiple SAs can be combined to create a single SA, enabling multiple
protocols to work together for enhanced security.

g.data-narration: Example combines ESP for encryption with AES-256-CBC and HMAC-SHA-256 authentication, supporting 128/192/256-bit keys.
IKE establishes and manages IPsec Security Associations using Diffie-Hellman key exchange and RSA for authentication.
g.text: IKE protocol establishes, manages, and maintains IPsec Security Associations
by negotiating key exchange and establishing IPsec SAs.

g.data-narration: IKE operates in two phases: Phase 1 establishes secure channel with main or aggressive mode, Phase 2 creates IPsec SAs for data protection.
The IPsec architecture includes AH for authentication, ESP for encryption, IKE for key management, and SPD for policy enforcement.
In summary, IPsec secures network communications through IPSP for policies, ESP for encryption, SA for protocol combinations, and IKE for key exchange.
IPsec enables site-to-site VPNs, remote access, is mandatory in IPv6, and secures sensitive government and enterprise communications worldwide.
